ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general chicken wing food product entry; boneless breaded deep-fried variant is not separately coded. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers coated deep-fried chicken meat; boneless wing-cut specific code not available. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for boneless breaded deep-fried chicken wings. Null assigned.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical individual serving of 6 pieces of boneless wings weighs approximately 200 g, providing around 580 kcal, 38 g protein, 35 g fat, and 28 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat only, coated, fried (SR Legacy ID 05062 / Foundation Foods equivalent)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input per_100g values (energy 290 kcal, protein 19 g, fat 17.5 g, carbohydrate 14 g, sodium 600 mg) are consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for breaded deep-fried chicken (SR Legacy: chicken, broilers or fryers, meat only, coated, fried). Values cross-checked against Singapore HPB Foodo database for fried chicken products. Input values retained as they fall within the expected range for this food type. Sodium at 600 mg/100 g is at the upper boundary for this category and is consistent with seasoned commercial preparations. Trans fat 0.25 g/100 g is plausible for commercial deep-frying. Calcium 15 mg, iron 1.0 mg, cholesterol 60 mg, vitamin C 0.0 mg retained from input and consistent with USDA reference data for this food type.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for boneless breaded deep-fried chicken wings. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the analogous food 'chicken nuggets / breaded fried chicken' from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). The wheat flour and breadcrumb coating is the primary glycaemic driver; the chicken meat itself has negligible GI contribution. GL per serving calculated as: GI (65) × carbohydrate per serving (28 g for 200 g serving) / 100 = 18.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 600 mg per 100 g — at the upper threshold for a single food component; cumulative sodium load across a full platter is extremely high
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should strictly limit portion size, request sauces on the side or choose low-sodium sauce options, and avoid soy-based or teriyaki sauces which add further sodium beyond the base 600 mg/100 g measurement.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined carbohydrate breading (14 g/100 g, fibre only 1 g/100 g) combined with sweet dipping sauces produces a high glycaemic and high-fat load; trans fat (0.25 g/100 g) worsens insulin resistance
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this food due to the high glycaemic breading, low fibre content, and presence of trans fat from deep-frying; if consumed, choose unsweetened spicy sauces and limit to a very small portion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Trans fat 0.25 g/100 g from deep-frying raises LDL and lowers HDL cholesterol; saturated fat 4 g/100 g and cholesterol 60 mg/100 g add further cardiovascular risk; high sodium exacerbates blood pressure
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this food due to the combination of trans fat, saturated fat, high sodium, and cholesterol, all of which independently and synergistically increase cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that worsen joint inflammation; trans fat (0.25 g/100 g) is strongly pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried breaded chicken due to AGE formation during frying and the presence of trans fat, both of which directly promote joint inflammation; grilled or baked unbreaded chicken is a less inflammatory alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boneless Chicken Wings (Deep-Fried, Breaded) is a popular Western-style fast-food dish sold widely in Singapore through delivery platforms and casual dining outlets. The dish consists of boneless chicken leg meat pieces that are coated in a seasoned wheat flour and breadcrumb batter, then deep-fried in oil until the exterior is golden and crispy while the interior remains juicy. It is typically served in multi-piece portions ranging from 6 to 100 pieces and is available in a variety of flavours including buffalo, honey, teriyaki, and spicy chilli. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 290 kcal, 19 g protein, 17.5 g fat (of which 4 g is saturated fat and 0.25 g is trans fat), 14 g carbohydrate, 1 g dietary fibre, and 600 mg sodium. The high sodium content, presence of trans fat from commercial deep-frying, and refined carbohydrate breading make this food unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. The glycaemic index is estimated at 65 (medium) based on the breaded fried chicken analogue from the University of Sydney GI Database, with a glycaemic load of approximately 18 per 200 g serving. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore outlets and contains gluten allergens from the wheat flour coating. It is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans. Nutritionally, it is a high-energy, high-fat, high-sodium snack food with moderate protein content and low micronutrient density.

Sodium in fried chicken wings Singapore

confidence_reason: Input per_100g values are consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for breaded deep-fried chicken and have been cross-validated against Singapore HPB Foodo reference ranges. The original data source is listed as GrabFood (a delivery platform, which is a forbidden primary source), so values have been independently validated against approved sources. Confidence is medium rather than high because no exact match for this specific commercial product exists in an approved primary source; values are adapted from the closest approved-source equivalent.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown, B-vitamin profile, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for this specific commercial boneless wing product are not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy or Singapore HPB Foodo database at the level of specificity required for this preparation. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): deep-fried breaded chicken contains no meaningful vitamin C; this is confirmed by USDA FoodData Central data for coated fried chicken products. All core macros (protein_g 19.0, fat_g 17.5, carbohydrate_g 14.0) are non-zero and nutritionally consistent with the food type. Sodium at 600 mg/100 g is at the upper boundary for this food category and reflects seasoned commercial preparation; sauce additions would increase effective sodium further. Trans fat 0.25 g/100 g is plausible for commercial deep-frying operations. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on breaded fried chicken from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). The original input listed multiple portions all marked is_default: true; only the 6-piece (200 g) portion has been designated as the default serving for nutritional display purposes. The 100-piece platter (3000 g) represents an extreme cumulative intake and is intended for group sharing. Halal status is based on input dietary_tags and is consistent with the majority of Singapore fast-food chicken wing outlets; individual outlet certification should be verified.
